Alright, now we're into step 10, which is headlines. We call headlines the entry point to your sales message. Which usually the first thing someone sees, even if they're skimming an ad that's printed, if they're watching a video, it's the first thing they're going to see. If they're listening to audio, it's the first thing they're going to hear. So the headline is like the front door to your message. And they won't come through the front door if they're not enticed, if they're not brought in. So the headline has a very specific job to do. To get good at headlines, one of the first things you want to do is collect headlines that you like. And we call them rips. But basically it's a good place to get your juices flowing. Look at good headlines, dissect them, break them down, find the common points with what you're doing. So that if you see a headline that you really like, say the one-legged golfer headline, then you somehow want to try that. Because you should be trying a lot of different headlines. We typically write 20 to 50 headlines, seriously, before picking one in a piece for a professionally written piece of copy. You don't have to write that many, but it's a piece of insight into how the top writers get that way. They spend a lot of time rewriting things and trying a lot of different angles with headlines. I'll show you some steps on how to do that. When you're dealing with other headlines that you've seen that you really, really like, and you think, I might want to rip this, that doesn't mean to steal it outright. What that means is to take the gist of it, take the angle that the writer had taken, and use it for your own. So you do have to break it down. You have to figure out what's common with what they used, if it was from a different market, say, and what is not common, what has to come out. So you replace the keywords with your own. So you decide what the keywords are and move on from there. Some suggestions. Early books, two early books that I found in my career helped me a lot because they had huge sections with nothing but examples of headlines. Now, Tested Advertising Methods by John Capels is an excellent book on advertising. It was written in the, I believe the 60s, I'm not sure, might have been the 50s. John Capels was a very famous ad guy who worked with David Ogleby. In fact, the award given out for the Direct Marketing Association is called the Capels Award for the Best Ad of the Year. So Tested Advertising Methods was the first book that I had. It was the first one I had dogged, and it just had a lot of examples of headlines, very old headlines going back to the 20s through the 30s, and nothing really modern. But for basic timeless stuff, it was a great place to start. One just as good as that is written by Victor Schwab, who wrote back in the 30s and 40s. His book is How to Write a Good Advertisement. I really suggest you have these two books on your shelf that you can refer to, and it's just full of headline ideas and headlines that you can rip from. During my career, these were the two books that I reference most often in my career. And then when you're ready to get to originals, when you start to get an idea, when you've looked at enough headlines, you looked at them critically, and you started to break them down and figure out what made this work, where's the magic sauce in this, where's the word that makes the most sense, then you can start writing your own. And I have a really good way for you to start doing that at the end of this section. So basically, there are two kinds of headlines. There are long headlines versus short. You will notice that a lot of the ones I've shown you have been long. They can sometimes be a couple of sentences. They can be a one long sentence with a lot of punctuation, like dashes and parentheses and things like that. But I've also done short ones, like one word headlines. So I consider them many stories, the long one, many stories and eloquent jabs, which would be very short testimonials, one, two, three words, something like that. So when appropriate, you can use a short headline or you can use a long headline. I prefer the long ones because for me, it's like the beginning of the conversation. So there's a lot of information I want to get out. But sometimes I have short ones too. The thing is that every word must earn its place. I strip down my headlines as ruthlessly as possible, excuse me, so that I get to the point where there isn't a single word left that I could take out that wouldn't dramatically change the meaning of the sentence or the phrase. So we're talking about no extraneous words at all. So I'm ruthless with adjectives and I try to get verbs to do a lot of the work. You'll see some examples as we get going. You will use what you learn about headlines for classic headlines and subheads. Subheads are, I will point them out there, the many headlines, the secondary headlines under the main headline in a written piece. They are also subheads that may break up the copy when you want to make something stand out in a book of chapter titles, the actual title of the book, chapter titles. In a VSL, in a video sales letter, it would be both the introductory statements. Maybe you have a headline over the video or you have some name for the headline or you break up the video and you have different sections in there. That would be using headlines too. Email subject lines are really many headlines. Speech topics can be a headline. How you title your podcast, what the subject is. Anything that requires a shortened sizzling, come hither blurb really uses what we use to make headlines as powerful as possible. All right, you really want to be the one thing he encounters today that shocks him out of his zombie state. I've repeated this several times. I repeat it because it's very, very important. You can do this by having a totally different attitude than everyone else, including him. Your prospect comes across your video or comes across your website or comes across your ad or whatever. You present a different attitude than he's feeling than anyone else has shown that in his day, say, everyone else in the magazine, everyone else online in that particular area he's been surfing, everyone else in his office as he's going through his day. Hooks, of course, are going to be very important and the rollicking tails. That's why we spent so much time on storytelling. That can take people out of that white noise state and into an aware state where they're receptive and they're able to change. They're able to accept the persuasive powers that you present to them to do what you want them to do. The incongruous juxtaposition of compelling sales elements again comes in. If you have that one-legged golfer or that small kid accidentally hitting the 420-yard drives, if you can work that kind of stuff in, then it's kind of the, whoa, what's going on here and that gets them into the conversation. Of course, the before and after shockers. We use the before and after thing. It's a great tactic to use for testimonials, as you learned. It's also a great thing for headline. Before I discovered X-Product, I was 100 pounds overweight. I was lost and lonely. I was dead broke. My business was failing, whatever. You can get those into the headline. Then, of course, there's inside information on the slide. Some examples might be, here's a headline. This is what's called a superscript. It's kind of a sub-headline, but it's called a superscript because it's over the main headline. It's old copywriter speak. Amazingly fun opportunity to catch up to and even pass everyone who's having better sex than you are right now. It's a free look, too. Then the main headline, the most exciting sex of your life, the most satisfying intimacy of couples who know. This is a sub-head. This is like a supporting actor to the headline. Life is too short to miss discovering why some couples always seem so much more in love and happier than everyone else. Respected doctors and sex therapists now agree. Deep lasting closeness is nurtured in the bedroom. Really ignited by simple sexual secrets hidden from most folks that can transform your love life almost overnight into something magical. Here's an example of a very short headline. Both of these pieces were written for Rodale and for those books that I've mentioned before, which were very conservative educational books for adults to actually make their lives better. There was nothing lurid or sensational about it. However, I made the ads a little bit sensational. Now it's your turn to finally discover for free why so many of the world's happiest couples insist that these explosives, that these explosive forbidden sex secrets are the key to quickly recharging the passion, intimacy, and super exciting romance in your relationship. This originally was all one headline. I broke it up and I realized that taking out the key element there was forbidden sex secrets, which all on its own stands as a very good eye catcher and alarming little piece of copy. Here's one I did for a self-defense client who was actually giving away training guns used by military and police training units. It was a gun. It didn't fire bullets, but it was a real gun. The talent was a world famous guy. A world famous street fighter will give you a free gun just to prove you can take it away from you, bare handed, as easy as candy from a baby. Actually in some of these headlines, the free gun was a lot bigger than the other parts of the headline. That's an example of a basic eye catching thing that's very, very short. Of course, there's a lot of feeling and emotion attached to the idea of a gun and things like that. When you have that advantage, you want to use it as much as possible. Never overlook the magic of who, what, when, where, why, and especially how when you're crafting headlines. The word how is especially important. Here's a classic one from the 30s. How to win friends and influence people. That was actually written, I believe, by Victor Schwab, who wrote it for Dale Carnegie's book, How to Win Friends and Influence People. This is an ad that I showed you before about the golfer. It starts, how does an outer shape 55-year-old golfer crippled by arthritis and 71 pounds overweight still consistently humiliate PGA pros, et cetera, et cetera. So it's a word how. I might have tried why in there. Why does this out of shape 55-year-old golfer still consistently humiliate pros? Or any of those things. The who, what, when, where, why, and how is a journalism standard. That's how writers learn to write news stories so they cover all of those instances early in the piece to give the most information in the shortest period of time. That's how good news stories are written. And here's a famous one from Gary Halbert. How to collect Social Security at Any Age. And this is the pro tip. Most top copywriters start out with a how-to headline and they work from there. What I mean is that they start with a headline that says how to do this, how to get this, how to acquire this, how to change this. Just starting with the how-to is very, very good. That's why there are whole sections in libraries and in the bookstores called the how-to section. You go in there, how to redo your own remodeling, how to change the oil in your car, how to do your own heart surgery at home with just a knife and a cigarette lighter. The how-to thing, it's overused quite a bit. So that actually places like Rodale and other top mailers who hire high-paid writers. They actually have it in the contract that you will not use how-to headlines because they think it's so overused. However, in most markets it isn't overused. It's just another tactic you can use. So for professional writers you want to move beyond the how-to headline, but you definitely want to start out with that and move from there. You can branch out from the how-to headline, but you start from there because it forces you to think how to do something. And it's a great starter tactic and now that I pointed this out, you're going to start seeing a lot of headlines out there to start with how-to. Okay, headlines. True or false? Running out of ideas. Okay, again, calling some questions. This one frequently pops up at this point in the class. When you're writing, do you write the headline first? In which do you write top-down at any point in your process? All right, there are two answers to that. One is occasionally I will write the headline first and write just from there and write linearly all the way through the ad. That is because I have a 30-year career doing this and I spent a lot of time doing it and I've internalized a lot of this stuff. For anyone without the 30-year career of being able to be very successful in that career, no, you don't need to write the headline first. And in fact, that's why we have it at the stage, we have it in the simple writing system. You got to go through your USP and get that down. You got to have your features and benefits down. Often the features and benefits that you write down and the bullets that you write down, one of the bullets will often become the headline. It's like, that's so good. That either becomes a headline or it becomes the gist of the headline or helps inspire a headline. So you want to cover all this other stuff, including the salesmanship mentality, the idea of who you're after and what's going on and getting into that particular groove that is going to best connect on the first punch. This is right out of the gate, the headline. So your reader may see it first, whether it's the subject line of a headline, whether it's a headline of a piece, whether it's the opening salvo of a podcast or a video sales letter. It's the first thing they see, but it's not the first thing that you write, not the first thing out of your head. You need to know what the hooks are. You need to know what all this other stuff is going on. The headline is almost the culmination of everything else you do up to this point. That gives you all the material that will go into the best headline you can come up with. Okay, thanks. How about using negative language or trying to get an initial negative response to using negative language in a headline or, I guess, we'll start with the headline question, but if you want to say something about anywhere in the copy, this might be a good time to sweep that up too. So there's a couple of ways to look at that. A lot of writers really shy away from having anything negative at all in their copy, whether it's at the end of the copy hidden in the middle or in the headline especially. I've never felt that need to make a rule about it. If being negative matters, if the urgency is so in your face to the reader where they know, like if they're, we talk about starving audiences. If your audience is starving virtually for something, starving for food, starving for shelter, starving to see the new Star Wars movie or something, you can go with negatives because the higher the passion index of your audience, the more they're responsive to anything that looks like a lead or a piece that is going to be about what they're very passionate about. So I would not shy away from negative unless I had been shown by testing or unless my audience was just not in a state where they're going to respond very well to it. So you have to get the zeitgeist to the market. You have to understand what's going on in your prospects head. And being negative doesn't mean you have to be nasty about it, although I've used nasty stuff too, just being upfront and being mean about it. I have the answer to the problem that's been haunting you in your entire life and no, I'm not going to share it with you. I actually used that in an email recently. No, I eventually shared it with them, but I was teasing. So all of those violate rules that old school conservative, super conservative advertiser always had because they were just terrified of offending anybody because they were afraid that if they got refunds or they got a complaint or somebody didn't give them a five star review on Amazon or any kind of negativity at all coming back from the marketplace just freaks them out. And as of course, really experienced veteran marketers know, you're always going to have some negative response to what you do. If you haven't got between a 10 and 15% refund rate, then you're probably not pushing hard enough out there in the world anyway. There are 15% of the population out there is just completely crazy anyway. So if you're not reaching people who are returning the product or complaining about details or just being kind of out of their mind, your stuff is not being read by everyone in your market. So you've backed way too far. So you're going to have a little pushback if you have the kind of advertising that wakes people up because a lot of people, even if you have what they need or what would make their life better, they're still going to resent the fact that you have it and you woke them up. They were slumbering quite nicely on the couch, so leading a life of absolutely nothing happening to them. Okay, thanks. You've mentioned you write, you said 2050, you write a lot of headlines frequently, you kind of get them out and then you pick one. Let's say your best guest, day one, or the client's best guest, how do you go about, like is it important to try other ones or test other ones or how much do you bank everything on that one? Yeah, it is. And the wonders of the web is how easy it is to test. Now there's different ways to do this and we're not going to get into the technical side of this. I think in the marketing rubble and Siders Club, certainly that's been discussed. Yeah, that's one of the areas. But yes, if you can't test, and we found, remember what Curly was telling us about single words that were changing. They found that a headline that got a so-so response, you changed a single word in there, and I can't remember exactly what the word was, but it was probably a verb or it may have been a noun or something, but that word quadrupled response. And a lot of ads that go out, that's a lot of ad words, you're very limited in what you can use with ad words. It's kind of a haiku poem there. So each word matters so much that if you've been using the wrong word or the word has perceived wrong or isn't read or it's boring and you find an exciting word that conveys more of the story, it can change response by a lot. You'll never know that by sitting back and thinking, I wonder if this is the word to use or this is the word to use. And by the way, those 20 to 50 headlines I write will sometimes be, I'll try a how-to headline and then I'll try the same headline with a when or why, and so make minor changes and do stuff. So if you look at the way I do a headline, I will write it out pretty much like a how-to headline, then I'll circle the important keywords or the words that matter the most. And then I will say, well, can I change that word or change this phrase or how does this affect things? So it might be the same headline with all kinds of other construction going on, almost like I haven't done this, but almost like laying an overlay on that and then trying just a couple of words different, laying another overlay over. I would caution you to do this by hand when you're getting a headline and then correcting it and use a pen and print it out and use a pen and do that because if you're just doing this in word or on your computer and you change a word, you don't know what the history is. So unless you're keeping close track, you don't know what the word you just replaced is, you don't know what you did five steps ago, you don't know how you changed it. So actually have the physical evidence of your change there. Your piece, after you've edited it a little bit, should look like the scene of a murder. There should be just ink everywhere. It should be a complete disaster, but you can still read it and you can actually tell where this happened and where that happened. Okay, and again, just something you blew through. Just for testing the technology, I don't care what you're doing. It is accessible and easy if you're printing postcards or you're sending emails or you're doing websites. Depending on your budget and your technology or your vehicle, how you test is going to vary, but it's accessible to everybody now. You don't have to send millions of pieces of banlish. You're doing billboards next to freeways. That's one of the few times you cannot easily split test that. And you had mentioned before in sales messages, two of the things to test would be headline and offer. If you just wanted to do like simple A-B tests as we call them, you can just change the headline and see if that works. Change the word in the headline, change the complete nature of the headline, which are highlighting there, change the hook, and then the offer is very important. Sometimes things are underpriced or overpriced and that's affecting sales. Yeah, we'll get to that. We've got a whole section on that, so we'll pick that one up then. How about a personalization in the headline? That's the thing people seem to have strong opinions on or questions about. Back in the old day when I was doing all direct mail and print advertising, we couldn't personalize in print advertising because that's magazines. You can't have 50 different magazines going out with 50 different names on. In direct mail though, technology caught up with the printing process and we were able to print out different names. People went nuts and we started putting names in the headlines. We started changing the, instead of dear friend, it would start saying hi Bob and then we'd put Bob's name in there five or six times in the thing. We were kind of crazy on it. At first it worked great and it still works and in certain markets it will work. However, with the way technology is now with email, personalizing email, having a personalized logic line, having a personalized saying hi Bob, how is Bob today? If Bob is looking for, they really did get that ridiculous after a while so that a lot of the marketers I know just stopped personalizing because so many people were doing it, doing it badly, that nobody was fooled into thinking, oh yeah, you know I'm Bob. I know a machine did this. But still in certain circumstances, personalization still does come in very, very handy. It's a tool that you should use sparingly but when you can use it and it makes sense, then it's definitely something that can help. Last question on this topic. Do you, you've mentioned you have a list of recommended power words. Is there such a thing as words in the real world you just can't use? There are ad networks, some of them don't like them. Email spam filters for example will flag them where you just have to have such a list of these are just no no words. Well I guess there are and as we've known especially in getting email delivered, sometimes using someone's name they'll come under suspicion red flags will pop up just using another marketers name. There could have been innocent when you wrote it and in the time between you sent the email out they suddenly became something that the filters were flagging. Certainly in words it's a different world now as far as using heavy duty slang or even swear words. I am as foul mouthed as a drunken sailor on leave sometimes when I write and sometimes that's okay and sometimes it's not okay. But as far as a master list you have to understand your market. If you don't understand your market and you don't understand how people get the information you're sending out, if you don't understand the nature of the vehicle you're using whether it's email, going through spam filters or whether it's US Postal Service being flagged for something you have to understand this stuff and there's way too much of it for us to explain here but so it's kind of a yes and no answer. But it's a new world now where a lot of words that used to flag things don't anymore. Yeah I would say that's probably, everything you're saying I agree with but all of that is so fluid now. As I remind people there is no Mr. Internet making all the rules. It's a series of disjoint fragmented servers running obsolete software and who knows what today's filter, you can't use that word. We'll even warn before we display the web page of that word and then the filter technology generally gets better and takes a step back but the word comes and goes. It's like you have to test it we would say but I would not encourage anyone to, if someone once told you if you put the word money or free in a headline somehow that's going to impact your results. It might but it probably won't. While many years ago I would have suggested reverse you'd look for an alternative. So like anything else really your results may vary but I don't think there is the concept of these words inherently cause trouble. It's market dependent it's not technology. I would just say that as far as headlines and stuff my two main books are kick ass copyright seekers of a marketing rebel and the entrepreneurs guide to getting your shit together. So those were I had people beg me not to use those headlines they both became best sellers and in fact almost everything I was warned would happen didn't happen and the opposite happened. They are much referred to they are on a lot of people's shelves and people are quite proud of them. Well done. Alright that concludes this section there's a short exercise that John is going to tell you about. Great. So here's your exercise for the headline section. I want you to write three how to headlines for your product for any product at all it really doesn't matter. The idea here is to get into the groove of writing a how to headline. So just three but get them down and put your all into it.
